Title: Transcriptomic Identification of Wheat AP2/ERF Transcription Factors and Functional Characterization of TaERF-6-3A in Response to Drought and Salinity Stresses

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: Promoter analysis shows that the TaERF-6-3A promoter contains multiple ABRE (ABA-responsive), MBS (drought-responsive), LTR (low-temperature-responsive), and W-box (WRKY-binding) cis-elements, suggesting responsiveness to ABA and various abiotic stresses. GeneMANIA-based protein association analysis indicated a network enriched for CBL/CIPK proteins, with AtCIPK22 experimentally confirmed to interact with TaERF-6-3A in yeast, leading the authors to speculate that a CIPK–TaERF-6-3A module may operate in wheat abiotic stress responses.
Plant_Genes.Summary_key_Findings_of_Core_Gene:
1. TaERF-6-3A is an AP2/ERF (ERF subfamily) transcription factor from wheat identified as drought-responsive in RNA-seq and qRT-PCR analyses.
2. TaERF-6-3A expression in wheat is induced by drought, salt, and cold stresses, and its promoter contains multiple ABA- and stress-related cis-elements.
3. TaERF-6-3A is a nuclear protein with transcriptional activation activity, mediated by its C-terminal region, and physically interacts with AtCIPK22 in yeast.
4. Overexpression of TaERF-6-3A in Arabidopsis thaliana increases sensitivity to drought and salt, reducing survival and increasing water loss relative to wild type.
5. Under drought and salt, TaERF-6-3A overexpression lines show higher MDA content, reduced proline accumulation, and lower activities of antioxidant enzymes POD, CAT, and SOD.
6. The expression of stress-responsive genes RD29A and P5CS1 and antioxidant-related genes POD1, CAT1, and Cu/Zn SOD is significantly downregulated in TaERF-6-3A overexpression lines under drought and salt stress.
7. The data support that TaERF-6-3A acts as a negative regulator of drought and salt stress tolerance, likely by repressing stress- and antioxidant-related gene expression and weakening antioxidant defenses.
Plant_Genes.Omics_Data: RNA-seq of wheat variety JW1 leaves under 0 h and 6 h drought stress; data deposited in NCBI SRA: SRR16077950, SRR16077951, SRR16077959, SRR16077956, SRR16077957, SRR16077958
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Negative regulator of drought and salt stress responses; overexpression in Arabidopsis thaliana increases sensitivity to drought and high salinity.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: TaERF-6-3A is a nuclear AP2/ERF transcription factor with a C-terminal transcriptional activation domain in yeast, and its overexpression in Arabidopsis negatively regulates drought and salt tolerance by repressing the expression of stress-responsive (RD29A, P5CS1) and antioxidant-related (POD1, CAT1, Cu/Zn SOD) genes, leading to reduced antioxidant enzyme activities and altered osmotic adjustment.
Plant_Genes.Upstream_or_Downstream_Regulation: TaERF-6-3A --(overexpression-associated repression of transcript levels)--> RD29A (stress-responsive gene) under drought and salt stress in Arabidopsis thaliana; TaERF-6-3A --(overexpression-associated repression of transcript levels)--> P5CS1 (proline biosynthesis gene) under drought and salt stress; TaERF-6-3A --(overexpression-associated repression of transcript levels)--> POD1, CAT1, Cu/Zn SOD (antioxidant-related genes) under drought and salt stress in Arabidopsis thaliana. The repression may be direct or indirect.
Plant_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: AP2/ERF transcription factor, ERF subfamily, containing a single AP2/ERF DNA-binding domain
Plant_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Nucleus; TaERF-6-3A-GFP fusion driven by CaMV35S localizes mainly to the nucleus in Nicotiana benthamiana leaf epidermal cells (confocal microscopy)
Plant_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: AtCIPK22 (calcineurin B-like interacting protein kinase 22, AT2G38490) from Arabidopsis thaliana physically interacts with TaERF-6-3A in a yeast two-hybrid assay
Plant_Genes.Expression_Pattern: In wheat JW1, TaERF-6-3A is induced by drought, salt, and cold stress. Under drought (20% PEG6000), expression rapidly increases, peaking at 1 h, then declines to a minimum at 12 h and recovers towards initial levels by 24 h. Under salt (200 mM NaCl), expression slightly decreases at 1 h, then increases to a peak at 6 h and declines thereafter. Under cold (4 °C), expression is unchanged at 1 h, then sharply increases to a peak at 6 h and gradually returns to basal level by 24 h. It is also upregulated by drought in RNA-seq and qRT-PCR analyses.
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In wheat JW1: drought stress induced by 20% PEG6000, salt stress by 200 mM NaCl, and cold stress at 4 °C (samples at 0, 1, 6, 12, 24 h). In Arabidopsis: drought imposed by withholding water for 10–12 days followed by re-watering for survival analysis, and salt stress by irrigation with 300 mM NaCl solution for 15 days.
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Abiotic stress tolerance (drought and salinity) mediated by AP2/ERF transcription factor TaERF-6-3A
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: abiotic stress (drought tolerance, salt tolerance, oxidative stress response)
Plant_Genes.Quantitative_Phenotypic_Alterations: After drought stress (no water for 12 days followed by 3 days re-watering), survival rates of TaERF-6-3A overexpression Arabidopsis lines are approximately 25–35%, significantly lower than the wild type survival rate of 78.3%. Overexpression lines exhibit higher leaf water loss rates than wild type under dehydration. Under 300 mM NaCl for 15 days, survival of overexpression lines is significantly lower than wild type (exact percentages not specified).
Plant_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: In Arabidopsis, overexpression lines show higher water loss rates from detached leaves and, under drought and salt stress, accumulate more malondialdehyde (MDA) and have reduced proline content and lower activities of antioxidant enzymes POD, CAT, and SOD compared with wild type, while no obvious differences are observed under normal conditions.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Wheat (Triticum aestivum) variety JW1 seedlings for RNA-seq, gene cloning, and stress-induced expression analysis; Nicotiana benthamiana leaves for transient subcellular localization assays; Arabidopsis thaliana ecotype Columbia-0 (Col-0) wild type and TaERF-6-3A overexpression (T3 homozygous) lines for functional assays under drought and salt stress; yeast (Y2H) strains for transcriptional activation and protein–protein interaction assays.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: RNA-seq of wheat variety JW1 under 0 h and 6 h drought to identify differentially expressed AP2/ERF genes; qRT-PCR validation of TaERF-6-3A expression under drought, salt, and cold in wheat; cloning of TaERF-6-3A CDS and construction of overexpression vector (pBI121-based); Agrobacterium-mediated transformation of Arabidopsis (floral-dip) to generate TaERF-6-3A overexpression lines; drought tolerance assays by withholding water and measuring survival and leaf water loss; salt tolerance assays using 300 mM NaCl irrigation and survival scoring; measurement of physiological and biochemical indices (MDA, proline, POD, CAT, SOD activities); qRT-PCR analysis of stress-related genes (RD29A, P5CS1) and antioxidant-related genes (POD1, CAT1, Cu/Zn SOD) in Arabidopsis; subcellular localization using 35S:TaERF-6-3A-GFP transient expression in Nicotiana benthamiana and confocal microscopy; yeast one-hybrid style transcriptional activation assays with TaERF-6-3A and truncations in pGBKT7; yeast two-hybrid assay testing interaction between TaERF-6-3A and AtCIPK22.
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: Identified as a differentially expressed AP2/ERF transcription factor from a drought-stress RNA-seq dataset of wheat variety JW1, then cloned from JW1 cDNA for functional analysis
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: TaERF-6-3A functions as a negative regulator of drought and salt tolerance, so reducing or knocking out its activity in wheat could enhance stress resistance by increasing antioxidant capacity and proline accumulation. TaERF-6-3A thus represents a candidate target gene for improving drought and salinity tolerance in wheat through transgenic breeding or gene-editing approaches, and the transcriptomic identification strategy illustrated here provides a route to discover additional negative regulators for breeding stress-resilient cultivars.
